title: TPU v1 · MXU-Centric Wide Datapath Instead of a General-Purpose NoC
oneLine: When one dataflow dominates a chip, a wide datapath that delivers full bandwidth plus a narrow control bus outperforms a general mesh NoC; true inter-chip networking (ICI) appears only when scaling across devices.

legend.name: Control bus
legend.desc: A narrow path carrying decoded CISC commands and handshakes; TPU v1 has only about a dozen instructions. Control and data are separated, and control traffic remains small.

legend.name: Wide datapath
legend.desc: The main on-chip interconnect: a very wide parallel path between the Unified Buffer and MXU that delivers a full activation row or partial sums each cycle. Its goal is lossless bandwidth, not flexible routing.

legend.name: Unified Buffer
legend.desc: A 24 MiB on-chip SRAM at one end of the datapath. Activations stream directly into the array and results return with almost no intermediate staging.

legend.name: MXU (convergence point)
legend.desc: The 256×256 systolic array is the single destination for dominant on-chip traffic. Because interconnect serves one compute core, the topology can remain extremely simple.

legend.name: ICI inter-chip interconnect
legend.desc: A single chip does not need a network. ICI and a 2D torus become relevant only when hundreds or thousands of TPU devices form a pod; this belongs to TPU v2/v3, not v1.

steps.cap: ① The host sends CISC commands over PCIe; the control unit decodes them and distributes signals on a narrow control bus, separating control from data.
② Activations move an entire row at a time from the Unified Buffer to the MXU over the wide datapath, sustaining bandwidth with almost no staging.
③ Weights arrive separately from off-chip DDR3. All data converges on the MXU, so general-purpose routing is unnecessary.
④ Partial sums and results return over the same wide path to the Unified Buffer for the next layer; one dominant flow needs only one dedicated link.
⑤ When one chip can no longer hold a model, connect multiple devices with an ICI 2D torus; networking appears at pod scale in TPU v2/v3.

before: First ask whether a network is needed: How many dataflows and endpoints communicate—arbitrary many-to-many traffic, or one dominant stream feeding one core? TPU is the latter, which determines whether a general NoC is justified.
Decisions to make: Topology (point-to-point link, bus, mesh, or torus), link width and frequency, separation of control and data, buffering and flow control, and provisions for inter-chip expansion.
Required inputs: Communication pairs, bisection bandwidth, and cadence from compute (§3.3) and memory (§3.4). For TPU, the Unified Buffer must deliver a full activation row to the MXU every cycle.
Hard constraints: Feed bandwidth, interconnect area and power, deterministic inference latency, and physical routability of an exceptionally wide bus.
during: Model the communication graph: TPU has one overwhelming on-chip flow, Unified Buffer↔MXU, with few nodes and a fixed pattern. A mesh NoC adds routers, latency, area, and power without useful flexibility.

Select the simplest topology: Since traffic converges on the MXU, use a dedicated point-to-point wide datapath rather than a routable packet network, eliminating routers, arbitration, and virtual channels.
Prioritize bandwidth over flexibility: Preserve the Unified Buffer's bandwidth all the way to the array with a very wide, short, nearby link. Flexible routing is not required.

Separate control and data: A narrow bus easily carries about a dozen CISC commands and handshakes; devote nearly all interconnect area and power to the datapath. This matches host-driven operation and predictable latency.
Accept the scalability tradeoff: A simple wide datapath does not scale across many on-chip endpoints. TPU keeps one chip minimal and delegates capacity scaling to a separate inter-chip layer.

Add networking only across chips: At pod scale, a real network is required. TPU v2/v3 use ICI with a regular 2D-torus topology to balance bisection bandwidth and physical layout; this is not a disclosed v1 feature.

result.label: On-chip interconnect
result.value: Wide datapath + narrow control bus (not a mesh NoC)

result.label: Traffic pattern
result.value: One dominant flow converging on the MXU

result.label: Datapath width
result.value: Very wide parallel path; one activation row per cycle

result.label: Control path
result.value: Host-driven; only about a dozen CISC commands

after: Validate bandwidth: Confirm on representative workloads that interconnect keeps the array full. If delivery limits utilization, widen the datapath or revise Unified Buffer bandwidth (§3.4).
Propagate physical requirements: Give backend design placement and routing constraints for the ultra-wide bus—keep it short and nearby—and provide timing budgets for its critical paths.
Establish deterministic latency: Verify that separating control and data yields a fixed, modelable datapath delay suitable for predictable data-center inference.
Plan the scaling layer: Treat single-chip non-scalability as an explicit tradeoff and assign multi-device pod connectivity to an inter-chip network such as ICI.
Add observability: Include bandwidth and occupancy counters to identify whether compute or interconnect becomes the real bottleneck.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (ICI/2D torus applies to TPU v2/v3 pods; general data-center inference-ASIC interconnect practice is noted separately.)
